channel.title: BinderBrief Daily
channel.description: Daily commercial insurance and underwriting automation insights.

item.title: How Manulife hit its first ROI on AI investment
item.description:
Manulife has achieved approximately $300 million in value from its AI investments toward a $1 billion goal by the end of 2027. The company deploys AI for personalized product offers, fraud detection, operational cost reduction, and decision support tools like Microsoft Copilot and proprietary generative AI assistants for its 38,000 employees. These AI implementations improve underwriting efficiency and reduce expenses, contributing to enhanced commercial broker margins and scaled operational capacity.
Name: Jodie Wallis, Company: Manulife, Email: N/A, Type: Individual, Info: Global Chief AI Officer responsible for Manulife's AI strategy and implementation Name: Michael Shashoua, Company: Digital Insurance, Email: michael.shashoua@arizent.com, Type: Individual, Info: Senior Editor and author of the article

item.title: When AI gets it wrong, the company holds the bag – and the insurance market is still catching up
item.description:
Gallagher’s national technology practice leader, Paige Cheasley, explained that companies deploying AI retain liability for AI-generated errors, data breaches, or reputational damage, regardless of fault attributed to the AI provider. This liability stems from the company's direct relationship with customers and parallels traditional professional liability issues, with insurance coverage such as professional liability expected to respond to AI-related claims, though policy language across lines remains unclear. The uncertainty in coverage and potential for multi-line claims affect carrier underwriting approaches and could influence broker margins due to evolving risk and coverage interpretations in AI-related insurance products.
Name: Paige Cheasley, Company: Gallagher, Email: N/A, Type: Individual, Info: National technology practice leader at Gallagher responsible for AI risk and insurance insights

item.title: Reliance Global builds AI insurance push with ex-Coinbase CTO
item.description: The article is not accessible. Please provide the text or content of the article for analysis.

item.title: Cheche Group rolls out AI agent to automate auto insurance renewals
item.description:
Cheche Group Inc. (NASDAQ: CCG) launched ABAO Agent, an AI-powered intelligent underwriting solution for auto insurance renewals, deployed at scale with 24/7 autonomous operation. The solution uses Cheche’s proprietary large language model integrated with core insurance workflows to automate full renewal processes including customer outreach, needs identification, policy follow-up, and conversion. This automation reduces labor and operational costs for carrier partners while enhancing underwriting efficiency and supports Cheche’s transition to an AI-driven insurtech model focused on intelligent risk management for new energy vehicle insurance.
Name: Lei Zhang, Company: Cheche Group Inc., Email: N/A, Type: Individual, Info: Founder, CEO and Chairman of Cheche Group Name: Crocker Coulson, Company: N/A, Email: Crocker.coulson@aumadvisors.com, Type: Individual, Info: Contact listed in the press release, likely associated with investor relations or advisory services for Cheche Group Name: Cheche Group Inc., Company: Cheche Group Inc., Email: IR@chechegroup.com, Type: Company, Info: China-based leading auto insurance technology platform focused on AI-driven insurtech solutions

item.title: How Insurers Know When It’s Time to Scale AI
item.description:
Carriers are advancing from AI experimentation to deciding when to scale AI initiatives, focusing on organizational readiness and business outcomes over technical capabilities. Insurers embed AI into core underwriting workflows, using platforms that enable underwriters to review AI decisions and build trust through oversight and data analysis. This integration improves underwriting efficiency and risk management while requiring insurers to adapt infrastructure quickly to remain competitive and comply with regulatory standards.
Name: James Thom, Company: Vertafore, Email: N/A, Type: Individual, Info: Chief Product Officer at Vertafore discussing organizational readiness for AI scaling in insurance. Name: William Steenbergen, Company: Federato, Email: N/A, Type: Individual, Info: Chief Technology Officer at Federato outlining AI’s role in underwriting decision-making. Name: Craig Weber, Company: Cognizant, Email: N/A, Type: Individual, Info: Head of Insurance Strategy at Cognizant commenting on AI adoption challenges and regulatory considerations. Name: Elizabeth Blosfield, Company: Carrier Management, Email: eblosfield@wellsmedia.com, Type: Individual, Info: Deputy Editor at Carrier Management and author of the article.

item.title: Global: AI, regulatory pressure and climate exposure top insurer risk agenda
item.description:
Global Insurance Law Connect published its eighth edition of the 2026 Risk Radar Report on 18 June 2026, identifying AI, regulatory pressure, and climate risk as top risks for insurers across five continents and 28 jurisdictions. The report shows these risks arise from AI-driven changes in underwriting, claims handling, governance, and regulatory scrutiny focusing on operational resilience and customer protection, alongside climate-related losses affecting pricing and product design. These factors are creating challenges for insurer underwriting efficiency, increasing regulatory compliance costs, and pressuring insurer capacity and risk affordability.
Name: Gillian Davidson, Company: Global Insurance Law Connect, Email: N/A, Type: Individual, Info: Chair of Global Insurance Law Connect overseeing cross-border insurer risk issues

item.title: Gallagher flags AI liability as next 'silent risk'
item.description:
Gallagher Insurance released a report highlighting AI liability as an emerging silent risk for New Zealand businesses, noting that 87% of organisations already use AI while governance and insurance frameworks lag behind. The report explains that AI liability risks often span multiple insurance categories, such as professional indemnity, cyber, and directors and officers liability, and arise mainly from governance gaps and over-reliance on AI outputs rather than technological failure. This mismatch in coverage and unclear accountability in AI supply chains threatens broker margins and carrier underwriting efficiency by complicating claims and prompting likely adoption of AI exclusion clauses similar to recent US market moves.
Name: Ben Carey-Evans, Company: GlobalData, Email: N/A, Type: Individual, Info: Senior insurance analyst commenting on insurer hesitation and liability uncertainty regarding AI

item.title: Roadzen’s AI Insurtech Bet Rides Margin Progress And Debt Relief
item.description:
Roadzen, Inc. (NASDAQ: RDZN) reported adjusted EBITDA loss reductions and reached an in-principle agreement to extend $11.5 million in senior secured notes to June 30, 2027. The company generates revenue from AI-driven telematics, claims automation, brokerage commissions, and on-demand mobility services across multiple regions, integrating proprietary driving data and AI to enhance underwriting and operational efficiency. These developments improve the company's near-term financing stability and support its potential for higher-margin brokerage growth and underwriting economics, impacting broker margins and carrier capacity positively if execution succeeds.
Name: Russell Burns, Company: N/A, Email: N/A, Type: Individual, Info: Financial analyst contributing market insights for Finimize Name: Theodora Lee Joseph, Company: N/A, Email: N/A, Type: Individual, Info: CFA and financial expert publishing investment research at Finimize Name: Reda Farran, Company: N/A, Email: N/A, Type: Individual, Info: CFA and contributor of economic and climate-related investment analysis for Finimize

item.title: Ireland’s ‘thriving’ insurtech sector ‘could be European leader’
item.description:
Ireland has more than 120 insurtech companies, a number comparable to Germany and France. The sector benefits from EU market access, a skilled workforce, and government support creating a favorable environment for technology development. This growth enhances broker offerings and carrier underwriting by expanding capacity and improving risk selection within European markets.
Name: Ben Margulies, Company: Lloyd’s List Intelligence, Email: ben.margulies@lloydslistintelligence.com, Type: Individual, Info: Reporter covering insurance and insurtech sectors

item.title: Insurance AI: What You Won’t Read in the Press Releases
item.description:
Symfa CEO Vitali Yurkevich reports that the insurance industry is experiencing widespread use of AI claims and underwriting tools that largely replicate existing carrier systems but struggle with real-world legacy integration. Vendors often fail to address the complexity of insurance workflows and institutional knowledge embedded in long-customized platforms like Guidewire, causing carriers to prioritize proven experience over marketing claims. This cautious approach reduces broker margins on speculative solutions while increasing carrier underwriting efficiency by favoring vendors with demonstrated domain expertise and operational deployments.
Name: Vitali Yurkevich, Company: Symfa, Email: N/A, Type: Individual, Info: CEO of Symfa, an insurance software development agency focused on digital modernization for property/casualty carriers, MGAs, and wholesale brokers.

item.title: Insurers Continue to Invest in AI, Says Evident AI Index -
item.description:
Insurers increased AI adoption over the past year, with Allianz surpassing AXA as the global leader in AI implementation, according to the 2026 Evident AI Index. This progress involves expanding AI talent pools, growing from a 32% increase in AI specialists despite an overall 2.2% headcount decline, and deploying agentic AI systems to automate end-to-end workflows in underwriting, claims, and fraud detection. These changes improve carrier underwriting efficiency and create measurable ROI, with firms like Manulife, Generali, and Intact Financial publicly reporting over $1 billion in combined projected AI value by 2030.
Name: Alexandra Mousavizadeh, Company: Evident, Email: N/A, Type: Individual, Info: Co-Founder and Co-CEO of Evident, leader in insurance AI benchmarking. Name: Christian Preece, Company: Evident, Email: N/A, Type: Individual, Info: Insurance Director at Evident, expert on AI adoption impact in insurance. Name: Alastair Walker, Company: Insurance-Edge.Net, Email: N/A, Type: Individual, Info: Journalist and magazine editor with 20 years experience covering insurance sector news.

item.title: Cyber market looks to general liability, D&O to address AI-related risks
item.description:
The article reports discussions at the Airmic conference regarding the treatment of AI-related risks in the insurance market, with senior cyber insurance sources favoring coverage under existing product lines such as general liability and directors & officers (D&O) policies. This approach involves integrating AI risk exposures into established underwriting frameworks rather than creating new standalone products. The decision impacts commercial brokers by maintaining current margin structures and carriers by optimizing underwriting efficiency without expanding capacity demands.
Name: Rebecca Delaney, Company: The Insurer, Email: N/A, Type: Individual, Info: Journalist reporting on insurance industry developments Name: Ricky Lamey, Company: Thomson Reuters, Email: ricky.lamey@thomsonreuters.com, Type: Individual, Info: Business development executive at Thomson Reuters Name: Tom Amber, Company: Thomson Reuters, Email: tom.amber@thomsonreuters.com, Type: Individual, Info: Global Demand Generation Marketing Manager at Thomson Reuters

item.title: Insurtech startups face squeeze as insurers build AI
item.description:
Aviva, Chubb, and MetLife are developing artificial intelligence capabilities internally, as reported by CB Insights, amid a decline in early-stage insurtech funding in 2025. These insurers are hiring roles focused on implementing AI tools to move beyond pilot phases, reducing reliance on external startups. This shift pressures commercial brokers and insurtech startups by tightening competition and limits alternative AI-driven service options, affecting margins and capacity for innovation.
Name: Staff Reporter, Company: Insurance Asia, Email: N/A, Type: Individual, Info: Reporter covering insurance industry news for Insurance Asia

item.title: Allianz tops AI index as insurers chase tech talent
item.description:
Allianz has been ranked number one in the Evident AI Index for Insurance 2026, leading 30 major insurers with an AI talent pool 28% larger than its closest competitor. The insurer integrates autonomous agentic AI systems across underwriting, fraud detection, claims management, and customer service, supporting over 900 active AI use cases globally. This scale of AI deployment improves underwriting efficiency and expands capacity, potentially increasing commercial broker margins by streamlining processes and reducing manual interventions.
Name: N/A, Company: Allianz, Email: N/A, Type: Company, Info: A global insurance company leading in AI adoption and integration.

item.title: Space startups seek insurance for orbital AI data centers
item.description: Space startups backed by major players including SpaceX and Blue Origin are exploring insurance for proposed orbital AI data centers, as coverage will be essential to attract the financing needed to build these expensive space-based computing platforms. While insurers already cover traditional satellite risks such as launch failures, orbital debris, and space weather, they have little historical data to price the unique risks of AI hardware operating in space, making underwriting a challenge. Industry experts expect the market to remain small in the near term, but early discussions with brokers and insurers suggest the sector is beginning to take shape.

item.title: Agentic AI in Insurance Claims: Where to Start, What to Automate, and What to Keep Human-Controlled: By Vital Soupel
item.description:
ScienceSoft published a detailed analysis on agentic AI deployment in property and casualty (P&C) insurance claims, highlighting examples such as Allianz’s Project Nemo and Travelers’ agentic voice service, with claims sectors adopting AI for intake, damage assessment, and fraud detection. Agentic AI coordinates multiple specialized AI tools into an automated workflow that can intake claims, verify coverage, assess damage, detect fraud, and escalate exceptions to humans, using layered controls and audit trails to meet regulatory requirements. The integration of agentic AI is reducing claims cycle time and loss-adjusting expenses, improving carrier underwriting efficiency and claims handling capacity while preserving human oversight for complex cases.
Name: Vital Soupel, Company: ScienceSoft, Email: N/A, Type: Individual, Info: Senior Insurance IT and AI Consultant at ScienceSoft and author of the agentic AI claims analysis Name: Vadim Belski, Company: ScienceSoft, Email: N/A, Type: Individual, Info: Head of AI and Principal Architect at ScienceSoft involved in agentic AI system design and regulatory compliance Name: Stacy Dubovik, Company: ScienceSoft, Email: N/A, Type: Individual, Info: Financial Technology Researcher at ScienceSoft, contributor to agentic AI research Name: Parminder Saini, Company: Triple Minds, Email: N/A, Type: Individual, Info: CEO of Triple Minds mentioned among Finextra contributors Name: Volodymyr Kuiantsev, Company: AKURATECO, Email: N/A, Type: Individual, Info: Co-founder at AKURATECO mentioned among Finextra contributors Name: Dave Rossi, Company: National Hunter, Email: N/A, Type: Individual, Info: Managing Director at National Hunter mentioned among Finextra contributors Name: Rajeew Vishvakarma, Company: Infosys, Email: N/A, Type: Individual, Info: Project Manager at Infosys mentioned among Finextra contributors Name: Sam Boboev, Company: Fintech Wrap Up, Email: N/A, Type: Individual, Info: Founder at Fintech Wrap Up mentioned among Finextra contributors Name: Somobrata Ballabh, Company: Lloyds Bank GmbH, Email: N/A, Type: Individual, Info: Risk Manager at Lloyds Bank GmbH mentioned among Finextra contributors

item.title: Earnix launches AIOS to reshape insurance decisioning
item.description:
Earnix has launched AIOS, an AI Orchestration System for Insurance, designed to integrate governed artificial intelligence across underwriting, claims, customer engagement, and retention processes. The platform operates above existing insurer technology stacks, connecting via open APIs to policy administration, underwriting platforms, CRM, and claims systems, and includes AI agents, workflow automation, model management, and governance controls. AIOS aims to improve insurer underwriting efficiency and decision-making speed, enhancing margins by embedding scalable, auditable AI across core insurance workflows without costly system replacements.
Name: Robin Gilthorpe, Company: Earnix, Email: N/A, Type: Individual, Info: CEO of Earnix, responsible for strategic direction of AIOS platform. Name: Harry Huberty, Company: Celent, Email: N/A, Type: Individual, Info: Senior analyst at Celent specializing in insurance technology and AI applications.

item.title: AI ambition is exposing hidden cyber weaknesses in M&A deals
item.description:
Aon’s head of transaction advisory services for EMEA, Ian McCaw, highlights that deploying agentic AI with elevated access to networks is reintroducing critical cyber vulnerabilities in M&A transactions, with risks transferring to new owners. Aon’s cyber diligence process evaluates exposed infrastructure, email security, and deep web intelligence to reveal hidden breaches and gaps in target companies’ cyber posture during transactions. This approach impacts deal valuations, warranty and indemnity insurance, and drives more cautious negotiation and underwriting practices, potentially reducing commercial broker margins and carrier exposure to undisclosed risks.
Name: Ian McCaw, Company: Aon, Email: N/A, Type: Individual, Info: Head of transaction advisory services for EMEA at Aon specializing in cyber risk in M&A deals

item.title: Peak3 Releases New Report on Insurance Modernisation -
item.description:
Peak3, a global insurance technology provider, released a report on multi-country insurance core modernisation highlighting cost savings and operational efficiencies for insurers operating across borders. The report explains that adopting a unified, cloud-native core platform with common APIs and aligned data models allows insurers to harmonise operations, standardise workflows, and deploy AI capabilities at scale across multiple countries. This approach reduces operational and IT costs by up to 70%, accelerates product development and regional rollouts, improves underwriting and claims consistency, and enhances the ability to compete in digital and AI-driven insurance markets.
Name: Adrien Lebegue, Company: Peak3, Email: N/A, Type: Individual, Info: CEO Europe & Middle East at Peak3, commenting on multi-country core platform adoption. Name: Bill Song, Company: Peak3, Email: N/A, Type: Individual, Info: Co-Founder and CEO of Peak3, emphasizing the importance of harmonised core systems for insurers.

item.title: Insurance AI hiring rises 32% despite workforce cuts
item.description:
Insurance companies increased their hiring of artificial intelligence (AI) specialists by 32% last year despite a 2.2% overall reduction in staffing, according to research by Evident. This growth in AI roles supports automation, analytics, claims, underwriting, and fraud detection through integrated agentic systems connecting workflows like first notice of loss, triage, and policy checks. The shift to enterprise-wide AI deployment improves underwriting efficiency and operational productivity while reducing manual bottlenecks, impacting how insurers allocate workforce resources and manage risk.
Name: Peter Sonner, Company: BeInsure, Email: N/A, Type: Individual, Info: Author of the insurance AI hiring article at BeInsure
